perturbation.original_text: 10. This Agreement shall remain in effect for a period of one (1) year from the effective date hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. This Agreement shall remain in effect indefinitely from the effective date hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies a one-year term, which is definite. Removing the definite term creates uncertainty around the agreement's duration, which is not allowed under contract law. The modified text makes the contract's duration indefinite, potentially violating the Statute of Frauds if it's interpreted as an agreement that cannot be performed within one year.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-701 requires certain agreements, including those that cannot be performed within one year from the making thereof, to be in writing. By making the agreement's duration indefinite, it could be argued that the agreement falls under the Statute of Frauds and is unenforceable because its terms are not clearly defined regarding duration, and performance may extend beyond one year.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract defined a specific term of one year. The modified contract changes the term to "indefinitely". The LLM explanation states that NY GOL § 5-701 requires agreements that cannot be performed within one year to be in writing. By making the agreement's duration indefinite, it could be argued that the agreement falls under the Statute of Frauds, and its terms are not clearly defined regarding duration, which may extend beyond one year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the requested entry could not be found. Thus, the agreement could be interpreted as violating the Statute of Frauds, depending on the specific circumstances and interpretation of the agreement. The change introduces a potentially significant legal issue, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to conflict of laws principles. The parties hereby consent and submit to personal jurisdiction of courts situated in New York County, New York, and all disputes arising in connection with the Agreement shall be heard only by a court of competent subject matter jurisdiction situated in New York County, New York.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to conflict of laws principles. The parties hereby consent and submit to personal jurisdiction of courts situated in Los Angeles County, California, and all disputes arising in connection with the Agreement shall be heard only by a court lacking subject matter jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies New York law and jurisdiction. Changing the governing law to Delaware and designating a California court lacking subject matter jurisdiction violates the principle that contracts must be adjudicated in a forum with proper authority. Also, the clause specifies that the selected court must be of competent subject matter jurisdiction and it contradicts the provision because the court lacks that jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1332 outlines the requirements for federal courts to have diversity jurisdiction, including requirements of minimum amount in controversy and diversity of citizenship. Designating a court 'lacking subject matter jurisdiction' directly contradicts the requirement that the court have the legal authority to hear the case, potentially making the forum selection clause unenforceable.

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that disputes will be heard by a court 'lacking subject matter jurisdiction.' This directly contradicts the fundamental requirement for a court to have the legal authority to hear a case. 28 U.S.C. § 1332 grants district courts original jurisdiction under specific conditions, implying that courts must possess the power to adjudicate matters brought before them. By designating a court 'lacking subject matter jurisdiction,' the contract directly undermines this foundational aspect of legal proceedings, creating an unenforceable and contradictory provision.

perturbation.original_text: 12. Any notice or other communication made or given by either party in connection with this Agreement shall be sent via registered or certified mail, postage prepaid, return receipt requested; by facsimile transmission, with confirmation of receipt; or by overnight courier service to the other party as follows:
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Any notice or other communication made or given by either party in connection with this Agreement shall be sent via telepathy to the other party as follows:
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies acceptable methods for delivering notice. Changing the method to telepathy is unreasonable and conflicts with the legal principle of reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-202 defines when a person has received a notification. It requires taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. Telepathy is not a reasonable method of communication, nor would it qualify as a method under ordinary course.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-202. Notice; Knowledge. (a) Subject to subsection (f), a person has " notice " of a fact if the person: (1) has actual knowledge of it; (2) has received a notice or notification of it; or (3) from all the facts and circumstances known to the person at the time in question, has reason to know that it exists. (b) " Knowledge " means actual knowledge. "Knows" has a corresponding meaning. (c) " Discover ", " learn ", or words of similar import refer to knowledge rather than to reason to know. (d) A person " notifies " or " gives " a notice or notification to another person by taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course, whether or not the other person actually comes to know of it. (e) Subject to subsection (f), a person " receives " a notice or notification when: (1) it comes to that person's attention; or (2) it is duly delivered in a form reasonable under the circumstances at the place of business through which the contract was made or at another location held out by that person as the place for receipt of such communications. (f) Notice, knowledge, or a notice or notification received by an organization is effective for a particular transaction from the time it is brought to the attention of the individual conducting that transaction and, in any event, from the time it would have been brought to the individual's attention if the organization had exercised due diligence. An organization exercises due diligence if it maintains reasonable routines for communicating significant information to the person conducting the transaction and there is reasonable compliance with the routines. Due diligence does not require an individual acting for the organization to communicate information unless the communication is part of the individual's regular duties or the individual has reason to k
perturbation.justification: The altered text replaces established methods of notification (registered/certified mail, fax, courier) with 'telepathy.' This directly contradicts UCC § 1-202(d), which states a person 'notifies' another by 'taking such steps as may be reasonably required to inform the other person in ordinary course.' Telepathy does not qualify as a reasonable or ordinary method of communication, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. The parties hereby acknowledge that disclosure of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof would cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party. Without prejudice to the rights and remedies otherwise available, each party is entitled to equitable relief through injunction if the other party or any of its employees, agents or representatives breaches any provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. The parties hereby acknowledge that disclosure of any Confidential Information in violation of the terms hereof would not cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party. Each party waives their right to seek equitable relief through injunction if the other party or any of its employees, agents or representatives breaches any provision of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that unauthorized disclosure would cause irreparable harm, and allows for equitable relief. The modified text claims that unauthorized disclosure would not cause irreparable harm, and explicitly waives the right to injunctive relief. This may violate the principle that certain remedies cannot be waived if they undermine the fundamental purpose of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 364 states that specific performance or an injunction will not be granted if damages would be adequate to protect the expectation interest of the injured party. However, the original clause acknowledges that the harm is irreparable, and injunctive relief would be appropriate. Waiving the right to injunctive relief contradicts the acknowledgment of irreparable harm. A blanket waiver can be seen as unconscionable when it undermines the very nature of protecting Confidential Information.

perturbation.justification: The original text acknowledges that disclosure of confidential information would cause irreparable harm, allowing for equitable relief. The changed text explicitly waives the right to injunctive relief, contradicting the acknowledgment of irreparable harm. The Law Explanation supports the idea that waiving the right to injunctive relief contradicts the acknowledgment of irreparable harm and can be seen as unconscionable when it undermines the very nature of protecting confidential information. The scraped snippets are not useful to evaluating this situation.
